summary: I spent part of my childhood around Jacksonville, Florida, especially South Jacksonville where there was a swimming hole I loved. I remember once being on a ferry boat going to Jacksonville when a storm capsized a dredge boat and eight people drowned. Also, when I was 14, I attended the Florida Baptist Academy which was located in Jacksonville at that time.

When I was 14 years old, I went to the Florida Baptist Academy in Jacksonville. It was a place where I studied and paid just one dollar a month. Later on, the school moved to St. Augustine and became known as the Florida Normal and Industrial Institute. I was active in Sunday School and was even elected as a delegate several times.

The Florida Baptist Academy I attended later moved to St. Augustine and was renamed the Florida Normal and Industrial Institute. Although I went there when it was still called Florida Baptist Academy, I know it is the same institution. It was an important part of my education and spiritual growth during my teenage years.

St. Augustine, Florida became significant to me because the Florida Baptist Academy relocated there and became the Florida Normal and Industrial Institute. Although I attended the school when it was still in Jacksonville, I followed its progress and knew it was now in St. Augustine. It represents an important chapter in my educational journey.

After my mother died in 1924, I went to St. Petersburg to stay with Bernice, my niece, for about three months. She took very good care of me during that time, and I did quite well there. Bernice was kind and did not want me to leave, but I felt I needed to return home.

After I married O.T. Millie, we moved to Melbourne, Florida. It was there that I experienced a difficult marriage, as he was very mean and abusive. Despite the hardships, Melbourne was the place where my family life took shape before I became blind and had to return home.

While traveling and performing music with my second husband, Henry Jackson, we spent some time in Washington, D.C. It was there that Henry bought me a guitar from Jess Jacobuck, which helped us continue our music career. Our time in Washington was part of a larger journey where we played concerts and earned money by performing on streets and in churches.

I think President Franklin D. Roosevelt is a good man who means well for people like us. I don't blame him for the way relief money is distributed because he can't see how we are treated. Even though times are hard, I believe he wants everyone to get their fair share of aid during these difficult years.

Though the transcript does not mention 'Precious Lord, Take My Hand' explicitly, music has always been a big part of my life. My husband Henry and I played and sang together, practicing late into the night and performing in many cities. Music was not only a passion but a way for us to make a living despite our blindness.
